coredata.dc:title: Z-scheme heterojunction and photothermal effect synergistic induced simultaneous removal and mechanism of Cr(VI) and RhB by GQDs/BiOBr/g-C3N4

coredata.dc:description: Heavy metals and organic dyes, such as Cr(VI) and rhodamine B (RhB), were usually both contained in printing and dyeing wastewater. Despite extensive research on the removal of RhB and Cr(VI) from wastewater, addressing the challenge of simultaneous removal of mixed pollutants remains a significant hurdle. In this paper, a Z-scheme heterojunction, denoted as GQDs/BiOBr/g-C3N4 (GBCN), was prepared from BiOBr and g-C3N4 with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) serving as electron transfer mediators. This composite exhibits a nanosheet morphology with an increased specific surface area of 7.58 m2/g. The synergistic effects of the coexistence system resulted in GBCN achieving complete RhB degradation within 100 min and an impressive 99.3 % photoreduction efficiency for Cr(VI). Density functional theory (DFT) calculations and experimental results suggested that the superior photocatalytic performance of GBCN was attributed to the highly efficient electron transfer and charge separation enabled by the Z-scheme heterojunction, in synergy with the electron transfer mediator and the photothermal effect. Quenching experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR) measurements validated the involvement of h+, O2 −, OH, and 1O2 in the oxidation of RhB, while e− contributed to the reduction of Cr(VI). Fukui index calculations revealed high f0 values for N2, N14, O23, and C23 of RhB molecules and the degradation intermediates of RhB were further identified by LC-MS analysis. Finally, the reduced toxicity of the product after RhB degradation was affirmed. This study offers new insights into the design of efficient Z-scheme heterojunctions for the simultaneous removal of organic dyes and heavy metal ions.

head.ce:title: Z-scheme heterojunction and photothermal effect synergistic induced simultaneous removal and mechanism of Cr(VI) and RhB by GQDs/BiOBr/g-C3N4

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heavy metals and organic dyes, such as Cr(VI) and rhodamine B (RhB), were usually both contained in printing and dyeing wastewater. Despite extensive research on the removal of RhB and Cr(VI) from wastewater, addressing the challenge of simultaneous removal of mixed pollutants remains a significant hurdle. In this paper, a Z-scheme heterojunction, denoted as GQDs/BiOBr/g-C3N4 (GBCN), was prepared from BiOBr and g-C3N4 with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) serving as electron transfer mediators. This composite exhibits a nanosheet morphology with an increased specific surface area of 7.58 m2/g. The synergistic effects of the coexistence system resulted in GBCN achieving complete RhB degradation within 100 min and an impressive 99.3 % photoreduction efficiency for Cr(VI). Density functional theory (DFT) calculations and experimental results suggested that the superior photocatalytic performance of GBCN was attributed to the highly efficient electron transfer and charge separation enabled by the Z-scheme heterojunction, in synergy with the electron transfer mediator and the photothermal effect. Quenching experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR) measurements validated the involvement of h+, O2 −, OH, and 1O2 in the oxidation of RhB, while e− contributed to the reduction of Cr(VI). Fukui index calculations revealed high f0 values for N2, N14, O23, and C23 of RhB molecules and the degradation intermediates of RhB were further identified by LC-MS analysis. Finally, the reduced toxicity of the product after RhB degradation was affirmed. This study offers new insights into the design of efficient Z-scheme heterojunctions for the simultaneous removal of organic dyes and heavy metal ions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The escalating urbanization and rapid industrialization have resulted in a significant influx of pollutants into water environments, causing the coexistence of multiple pollutants. Among them, the most typical is the coexistence of organic dyes and heavy metal ions in printing and dyeing wastewater. Residual organic dyes in water adversely impact aquatic organisms and transform into carcinogenic substances upon entering the human body, causing irreversible health effects [1]. Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) is highly soluble and extremely toxic, with the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) setting a regulatory limit for Cr(VI) in drinking water at <0.1 mg/L [2]. In contrast, trivalent chromium (Cr(III)) is non-toxic and serves as an essential trace element. Consequently, the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) stands out as an ideal method for removing Cr(VI) from water environments. Despite extensive research on the removal of organic dyes and Cr(VI) from water environments in recent years, existing studies primarily focus on the removal of single pollutants, overlooking the practical challenge posed by the coexistence of organic dyes and Cr(VI) [3,4]. Among several existing methods for removing mixed pollutants, photocatalysis is a cost-effective and safe method for the purification of water bodies [5]. It is driven by visible light and uses holes (h+) generated by light excitation of semiconductor materials to oxidatively decompose organic dyes, while electrons (e−) reduce Cr(VI) to Cr(III) to achieve synergistic removal of two pollutants [6,7]. The core of photocatalytic technology is to construct catalysts with high catalytic activity. In addition, the photothermal effect possessed by special semiconductor materials can increase the surface temperature and further increase the migration rate of photogenerated carriers, thus achieving effective removal of pollutants [8–10]. Based on the above characteristics, photocatalysis is expected to achieve simultaneous removal of organic dyes and Cr(VI) in coexisting systems [11].

Single-component semiconductor materials face challenges in achieving a robust redox capability and a broad visible light absorption range, primarily due to the rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers and holes. Various strategies have been explored to enhance the photocatalytic performance of single-component semiconductor materials, encompassing elemental doping [12], interfacial modulation [13], and heterostructure construction [14]. Among these methods, the construction of heterojunctions has proven effective in improving the separation performance of photogenerated carriers and optimizing the redox properties of each semiconductor material [15]. Z-scheme heterojunctions, in particular, offer simultaneous advantages in achieving efficient charge separation and a strong redox capability. This effectiveness is attributed to the unique energy band structure, which allows electrons to transfer from a semiconductor with a more positive conduction band (CB) potential to another semiconductor with a more negative valence band (VB) potential. This transfer maximizes the utilization of electrons from the semiconductor with a more negative CB in the reductive properties and the semiconductor with a more positive VB in the oxidative properties of the holes, effectively suppressing the recombination of electrons and holes [16].

In recent years, graphitic phase carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has garnered attention as a photocatalyst due to its distinctive optical properties, simple preparation method, and favorable band gap (~2.7 eV) [17]. However, single-component g-C3N4 encounters challenges, including a high rate of photogenerated electron-hole (e−-h+) recombination and a limited visible light absorption range. On the other hand, BiOBr, among bismuth-based materials, is known for its appropriate band gap (~2.8 eV), and well-positioned valence and conduction bands [18]. However, the practical application of pure BiOBr is also hindered by a high rate of photogenerated carrier complexation. Z-scheme heterojunctions formed by combining g-C3N4 and BiOBr have exhibited exceptional photocatalytic performance due to the complementary layered structure and energy band alignments of both materials [19]. Nevertheless, direct Z-scheme heterojunctions still present areas for improvement, particularly in terms of electron transport and charge separation. Recent studies suggest that a Z-scheme heterojunction, connecting two semiconductors with an electron transfer mediator, further enhances photocatalytic performance by effectively separating photogenerated carriers and accelerating the electron transport rate [20]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, stand out for their nanoscale dimensions, low toxicity, and controllable preparation conditions. The π-conjugated structure of GQDs acts as an electron acceptor, facilitating the transfer of photogenerated electrons and extending the lifetime of carriers. Additionally, GQDs exhibit light capture over a broader wavelength range through upconversion luminescence properties, producing notable photothermal effects [21]. In our previous study, GQDs with abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on their surface were successfully prepared via a hydrothermal method [4]. These functional groups foster strong interactions or chemical bonds with polar organic molecules. Leveraging these properties, utilizing the prepared GQDs as an electron transfer mediator is anticipated to effectively enhance the electron transfer capability of Z-scheme heterojunctions.

In this paper, GQDs/BiOBr/g-C3N4 composites were prepared by a combination of calcination, hydrothermal, and deposition methods for photocatalytic simultaneous removal of RhB and Cr(VI). This work aims to elucidate the issues: (i) investigating the morphological, chemical, and physicochemical characteristics of the composites through diverse characterizations such as SEM, XRD, FT-IR and XPS; (ii) the catalysts prepared showed 100 % and 99.3 % removal of RhB and Cr(VI), respectively, and the effects of inorganic anions, natural organic matter and pH on the reaction performance were systematically investigated; (iii) proposing potential photocatalytic synergistic removal and photothermal catalytic mechanisms, supported by experiments and DFT calculations; (iv) identifying the reaction sites of actives on RhB using Fukui index calculations and analyzing potential degradation pathways through three-dimensional fluorescence and LC-MS; (v) predicting changes in toxicity during RhB degradation. This work serves as a valuable reference for the development of environmentally friendly catalysts capable of simultaneous removal of organic dyes and heavy metals from aqueous environments.
The chemicals and reagents used are detailed in Text S1 of the Supplementary Materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs, BiOBr and g-C3N4
ce:section.ce:para: Individual GQDs, BiOBr, and g-C3N4 components were synthesized following established procedures outlined in Text S2 of the Supplementary Materials.

The composite preparation process is illustrated in Fig. S1. Initially, a solution (referred to as solution A) was formulated by dissolving 2 mmol of KBr and 4 mmol of anhydrous sodium acetate in 24 mL of deionized water. Subsequently, various masses of g-C3N4 (100 mg, 200 mg, 300 mg, 400 mg) were introduced into the solution, followed by 30 min of stirring after 1 h of sonication. Concurrently, another solution (referred to as solution B) was prepared by ultrasonically dissolving 2 mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O in 1.5 mL of glacial acetic acid. Solution B was then gradually added dropwise to solution A, with the color of the resulting solution transitioning from dark yellow to light yellow. After an additional 30 min of stirring, 5 mg of GQDs were introduced, and stirring continued for 24 h at room temperature. The obtained samples were washed three times with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol, followed by filtration. The final step involved drying the samples at 60 °C for 24 h. The obtained composites, distinguished by different masses of g-C3N4, were designated as GBCN-100, GBCN-200, GBCN-300, and GBCN-400, respectively.
Detailed information on all characterizations can be found in Text S3 of the Supplementary Materials.

The photocatalytic activity and photothermal effect experiments of the prepared catalysts for the simultaneous removal of RhB and Cr(VI) was investigated by irradiation under a 300 W Xe lamp (CEAULIGHT, CEL-HXF 300 E7). A 400 nm cut-off filter was used to adjust the wavelength to visible light from 400 to 780 nm, intensity 280 mω/cm2 as measured by a digital light power meter (CEL-NP2000-2). In each photocatalytic and photothermal effect experiment, 150 mg of the catalyst was introduced into a mixed solution comprising 100 mL of K2Cr2O7 (40 mg/L) and 100 mL of RhB (40 mg/L) and 3 mM of citric acid, with a photocatalyst concentration of 0.75 g/L. The mixture was then stirred continuously in the dark for 30 min. Subsequently, the solution was exposed to visible light for 100 min, sampled at regular intervals, and filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. The absorbance of RhB was measured at λ = 554 nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-1900). The concentration of Cr(VI) in the supernatant was determined through a standard diphenylcarbazide (DPC) method (Text S3). The removal rate (RR) is calculated according to the following formula:

where C is the concentration at a given reaction time (mg/L), C0 is the initial concentration (mg/L), and k is the apparent rate constant (min−1) for RhB degradation and Cr(VI) reduction.

In the cycling test, GBCN-300 in the post-reaction solution was separated from the suspension, subjected to multiple washes with ultrapure water and anhydrous ethanol, and subsequently filtered. The resultant material was then vacuum-dried at 60 °C for 12 h for subsequent test repetitions. All experiments were iterated three times, and the mean value was considered for analysis.

DFT calculations of RhB were conducted employing Gaussian 16 software. The geometry of RhB was optimized using the B3LYP generalization under the 6-31 + G(d, p) basis set. The Fukui index was derived from the Hirshfeld charge of RhB. Work functions and density of states calculations were performed using the Cambridge Serial Total Energy Package (CASTEP) method within the Materials Studio (MS) program, employing the principles of DFT. Detailed information on these calculations is provided in Text S4.

SEM was employed to analyze the morphology of g-C3N4, BiOBr, and the GBCN composite. The SEM image of g-C3N4 depicted a block woth a rough surface, characteristic of melamine polymerization [22]. BiOBr exhibited a nanoflower morphology (Fig. 1(b)), while GBCN composites were composed of rough irregular nanosheets (Fig. 1(c)). This modification is expected to increase the contact area between the active groups on the surface of the material and the contaminant, thus potentially improving the removal of the contaminant by the material. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images confirmed the cohesive binding of GQDs, BiOBr, and g-C3N4. The high-resolution TEM image of GBCN revealed the presence of (100) crystal faces of GQDs, (102) crystal faces of BiOBr, and (002) crystal faces of g-C3N4. Energy dispersive X-ray spectra (EDS) demonstrated a uniform distribution of elements (Fig. 1(g)), further confirming the successful preparation of ternary GBCN photocatalysts.

XRD analysis revealed the crystalline phase structures of the photocatalysts. The diffraction peaks of BiOBr precisely matched those of the tetragonal phase standard card (JCPDS No. 78-0348) [23]. The characteristic peaks of g-C3N4 were consistent with the reported g-C3N4 structure (JCPDS No. 87-1526), and the peaks located at 13.1° and 27.5° corresponded to the (100) crystallographic facet caused by interlayer stacking of the triazine structure and the (002) crystallographic facet associated with interlayer stacking of the graphitic material, respectively [24].The intensity of the g-C3N4 diffraction peak is weak in the GBCN composites, which may be due to the fact that BiOBr covers its surface and blocks the arrival of X-rays [25]. According to the XRD results, the introduction of g-C3N4 and GQDs did not destroy the original crystalline structure of BiOBr, resulting in a higher stability of the composites.

FT-IR was employed to examine the surface chemical bonding of the materials (Fig. 1(i)). In g-C3N4, peaks within the 3000–3300 cm−1 region correspond to NHx, and characteristic peaks within the 1193–1626 cm−1 range are attributed to the stretching vibrations of the CN heterocycles [26]. The presence of the typical triazine structure in g-C3N4 is indicated by the characteristic peak at 808 cm−1 [27], while the peak at 887 cm−1 corresponds to an aberration of the NH amino group [28]. For BiOBr, the characteristic peaks near 770 and 1285 cm−1 are associated with typical BiO stretching vibrations [29], and the peak at 1614 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibration of the OH bond [30]. In the GBCN composites, the intensity of the characteristic peaks is weakened, indicating the interaction between g-C3N4 and BiOBr and the better photocatalytic activity of the composites.

XPS was employed to analyze electron transfer at the heterojunction interface and the chemical state of the composite material. As depicted in Fig. S2, the composite material was composed of elements Bi, Br, O, C, and N, consistent with the EDS results. High-resolution Bi 4f XPS spectra (Fig. 2(a)) exhibited characteristic peaks at 159.13, 159.10 eV (Bi 4f7/2) and 164.54, 164.44 eV (Bi 4f5/2) for both BiOBr and GBCN [4]. Similarly, the XPS spectra of Br 3d displayed characteristic peaks at 68.13 and 69.18 eV for Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2 in BiOBr, and corresponding peaks at 68.20 and 69.23 eV in GBCN [31]. The O 1s spectra of both composite GBCN and BiOBr exhibited characteristic peaks associated with BiO bonds and oxygen vacancies [12]. The C 1s spectra of the GQDs revealed peaks at 284.15, 284.80, 285.95, and 287.11 eV, corresponding to CCa, CC/CC, COH, and HOCO, respectively [4]. While the C 1s spectrum of g-C3N4 showed three characteristic peaks at 284.80, 288.04, and 293.42 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, NCN, and π excitation, respectively [32,33]. In the composite, the C 1s spectrum closely resembled that of g-C3N4, with the disappearance of the π-excitation at 293.42 eV and a shift in the characteristic peak corresponding to NCN to a higher binding energy. The N 1s spectrum of g-C3N4 revealed characteristic peaks at 398.44, 398.94, 400.56, and 404.04 eV, attributed to the sp2 structure, amino nitrogen, bridging nitrogen, and π-π* excitation, respectively [34,35]. Overall, the N 1s spectra of the composites show a shift in the peaks as do the Bi 4f, Br 3d and O 1s spectra compared to the individual components, suggesting stronger interactions within the materials, resulting in higher photocatalytic activity and stability of the composites [36].

The specific surface area and pore structure of the catalysts were systematically investigated using N2 adsorption-desorption curves, and the detailed data are shown in Table S1. As illustrated in Fig. 2(f), the isotherms for BiOBr, g-C3N4, and GBCN all exhibited type IV isotherms with an H3 hysteresis loop, indicating mesoporous structures. The specific surface areas of g-C3N4 and BiOBr were 6.95 and 6.99 m2/g, respectively, with GBCN showing a slight increase to 7.58 m2/g. While there was a modest increase in the specific surface area of the composites, the differences in specific surface area, pore volume, and pore size were not substantial among the three materials, suggesting that these factors did not predominantly influence their photocatalytic activity.

The hydrophilic characteristics of the photocatalytic material were assessed by measuring water contact angles. In Fig. 2(g) and (h), g-C3N4 and BiOBr exhibited static water contact angles of 106.1° and 119.3°, confirming their hydrophobic nature. In contrast, the composite GBCN displayed a significantly reduced water contact angle of 50.2° (Fig. 2(i)), indicating strong hydrophilicity. This attribute promotes the dispersion of GBCN in aqueous solutions, enhancing its interaction with RhB and Cr(VI) during the photocatalytic process.

The catalytic efficacy of GBCN composites removing RhB and Cr(VI) was evaluated in both single and coexistence systems (Fig. S3). In the individual pollutant presence system, Cr(VI) removal rate reached 55 %, while in the coexistence system with RhB, Cr(VI) removal rate significantly increased to 99.3 %. Simultaneously, RhB degradation rate in the coexistence system reached 100 %. The apparent rate constants (k) for Cr(VI) and RhB in the coexistence system were 3.67 and 7.11 times higher, respectively, compared to the single system. These results highlight a notable synergistic effect, with RhB and Cr(VI) acting as carrier trapping agents for each other, enhancing the efficient utilization of photogenerated carriers. Given the above findings, the prepared composites exhibited a great advantage for the removal of mixed pollutants, and therefore the subsequent investigation was centered on the simultaneous removal of RhB and Cr(VI) in the coexistence system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous removal of RhB and Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic performance of catalysts for the synergistic removal of RhB and Cr(VI) under visible light irradiation was investigated. Fig. 3(a) demonstrates that the removal of both pollutants within a system with the presence of both a hole trapping agent and a catalyst is significantly better than that in other systems. In the presence of GBCN and CA in the coexisting system under dark conditions, the removal of pollutants for both was around 15 %. Under light conditions, the removal rates of pollutants were higher than 99 %, demonstrating that the active substances in the system play a major role and the photocatalytic carrier is of minor significance. In Fig. 3(b), the k value of Cr(VI) in the system with a hole trapping agent and a catalyst is 104 times higher than that of the system with light alone. The introduction of a hole trapping agent hinders the degradation of RhB, whereas other active components in the system were also effective in degrading RhB. As illustrated in Fig. 3(c) and (d), RhB and Cr(VI) were synergistically removed by various catalysts. Among them, the photocatalytic performance of GBCN-300 was significantly improved relative to g-C3N4 and BiOBr, with the removal rate of both pollutants exceeding 99 %. Fig. 3(e) confirms that GBCN-300 outperforms previously reported photocatalysts for simultaneous RhB and Cr(VI) removal [37–43].

The removal efficiencies of the prepared photocatalysts for TOC during the simultaneous photocatalytic RhB and Cr(VI) are presented in Fig. 3(f). The TOC removal rates of BiOBr and g-C3N4 alone were below 10 %, whereas the composite GBCN exhibited the highest TOC removal rate of 49.73 % in the system. Overall, the GBCN photocatalytic RhB/Cr(VI) system demonstrated a superior mineralization rate for RhB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of various factors on photocatalytic performance
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. S4(a) and (b), the efficiency of photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction and RhB oxidation were gradually increased with the addition of catalyst dosage, reaching a peak at 0.75 g/L. However, when the catalyst concentration was further increased to 1.25 g/L, the removal rates of two pollutants in the coexistence system decreased due to excess catalyst reducing light absorption, and diminishing photocatalytic efficiency [44]. Various hole trapping agents were introduced into the RhB/Cr(VI) coexistence system, revealing a significant improvement in Cr(VI) photoreduction. In particular, citric acid achieved a remarkable efficiency of 99.3 %, attributed to its facile adsorption on the composite surface and capture of h+ in the electron-hole pairs [45]. Investigation of different citric acid dosages confirmed that a moderate amount effectively promoted Cr(VI) reduction within the coexistence system. The highest photocatalytic synergistic removal efficiency of RhB and Cr(VI) by GBCN was attained with a catalyst dosage of 0.75 g/L and citric acid dosage of 3 mM.

In the investigation of different concentrations of RhB on the photocatalytic synergistic removal of RhB and Cr(VI), the initial concentration of K2Cr2O7 was set to be 20 mg/L. The results showed that RhB and Cr(VI) were completely removed within 100 min when the concentration of RhB was 5–20 mg/L. However, at higher RhB concentrations (25 and 30 mg/L), RhB degradation efficiency decreased to 86.6 % and 84.7 %, with a slight decrease in Cr(VI) reduction efficiency (Fig. S5(a) and (b)). With the initial RhB concentration of 20 mg/L, it was observed that alterations in Cr(VI) exerted limited influence on RhB, whereas the reduction of Cr(VI) exhibited notable variations. Lower K2Cr2O7 concentrations reached near-complete photoreduction equilibrium quickly, but at 25 and 30 mg/L, Cr(VI) removal rate decreased to 85.1 % and 81.1 %, respectively (Fig. S5(c) and (d)). Overall, higher initial pollutant concentrations resulted in insufficient free radicals being quantified in the system for pollutant removal, leading to lower removal rates [46].

As shown in Fig. 3(g), acidic conditions were more favorable for the synergistic reduction of Cr(VI), while alkaline conditions severely hindered the reduction of Cr(VI). Based on the species distribution of Cr(VI) under different pH conditions, it is obvious that the main form of Cr(VI) is HCrO4 − under acidic conditions (pH < 7), while it mainly exists in the form of CrO4 2− under alkaline environment (pH > 7). Under acidic conditions, excess H+ favors the reaction of HCrO4 − to form Cr3+ (Eq. (3)). However, excess OH− reacts with CrO4 2− to form insoluble substances under alkaline conditions (Eq. (4)), preventing the formation of Cr3+. Besides, the zeta potential of GBCN-300 at different pH showed that its zero-point charge was 7.24. Therefore, under acidic conditions, the catalyst surface was positively charged, which was favorable for the adsorption of HCrO4 −. However, the negatively charged GBCN-300 was unfavorable for CrO4 2− reduction due to the electrostatic repulsion effect under alkaline conditions. Regarding RhB degradation in the synergistic system, both acidic and alkaline conditions proved unfavorable. Interestingly, when the pH of the system was higher than 13, RhB degradation was nearly impossible due to excess OH− scavenging active substances. However, when the range of pH was from 1 to 9, the degradation rate of RhB was higher than 70 %, with optimal removal achieved under the neutral condition. In conclusion, the photocatalytic synergistic removal of RhB and Cr(VI) by GBCN-300 was suitable for a wide range of pH values, and a high removal rate can be achieved without adjusting the pH value of the solution.

The impact of inorganic salts (NaCl, Na2SO4, NaHCO3, NaH2PO4) and natural organic matter (humic acid (HA), fulvic acid (FA)) on the photocatalytic performance of GBCN was investigated in mixed solutions containing RhB and Cr(VI). Fig. 3(h) showed the addition of all these substances hindered the removal of RhB and Cr(VI). During the degradation of RhB, Na2SO4 showed the least inhibitory effect, while the addition of HA inhibited RhB degradation to a maximum extent, reducing the RhB removal rate to 81.4 %. In Cr(VI) reduction, NaCl and Na2SO4 had a minor impact, while the reaction of H2PO4 − and HCO3 − with the active substances also reduced the photocatalytic activity to some extent. It is noteworthy that HA exerted a significant inhibitory effect on Cr(VI) reduction, probably due to its reduced visible light transmission and its involvement in quenching reactions with reactive species [47]. Overall, GBCN demonstrated robust adaptability to inorganic anions and natural organic matter.

To evaluate the degradation efficacy of the photocatalyst GBCN-300 for diverse organic dyes in a coexistence system, a mixture containing 20 mg/L K2Cr2O7 and 20 mg/L common dyes (Congo red (CR), methyl orange (MO), methylene blue (MB), bromophenol blue (BPB)) was exposed to visible light. GBCN-300 exhibited notable removal capability for Cr(VI) in the presence of other dyes. In the coexistence system with MO and Cr(VI), removal rates were 85.8 % for MO and 83.8 % for Cr(VI). Meanwhile, removal rates for MB, CR, and BPB exceeded 85 %, with Cr(VI) removal rate reaching approximately 90 %. These results highlight the effectiveness of GBCN-300 in the simultaneous removal of various organic dyes and Cr(VI), showcasing its potential for diverse pollutant scenarios.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and reusability of the materials
ce:section.ce:para: The stability and reusability of GBCN-300 in the photocatalytic removal of RhB and Cr(VI) were assessed over five cycles, revealing a marginal reduction to 82.2 % for RhB and 86.3 % for Cr(VI) in performance. This slight decline may be attributed to incomplete cleaning of the active site, causing it to be covered by RhB intermediates and Cr(III). FT-IR and XRD analyses after five cycles demonstrated unchanged characteristic diffraction peaks, confirming the stability of the GBCN-300 in simultaneous pollutant removal. XPS spectra indicated minimal alterations in Bi, Br, O, C, and N peaks, further affirming its stability. Additionally, Fig. 4(d) depicted characteristic peaks of Cr(VI) 2p in the solution before the reaction, whereas characteristic peaks attributed to Cr(III) 2p at 585.78 eV and 576.05 eV emerged after the reaction [48]. This observation confirms the reduction of highly toxic Cr(VI) to less toxic Cr(III) during the photocatalytic process and the efficacy and stability of GBCN-300 in pollutant removal.

To validate the presence and impact of the photothermal effect on the simultaneous photocatalytic removal of RhB and Cr(VI), in-situ temperature measurements were conducted on the photocatalysts at various irradiation intervals. As shown in Fig. 5(a), the surface temperatures of g-C3N4 and BiOBr increased from 28.4 °C and 21.4 °C to 40.4 °C and 34.7 °C after 15 min of irradiation, respectively. Similarly, the surface temperature of the GBCN exhibited a remarkable rise, reaching 55.1 °C within 1 min and 62.7 °C within 15 min. Heat in the photocatalytic process originates from two primary sources: thermal radiation from direct light exposure and the inherent photothermal effect of the material. The thermal change caused by external light is constant, so the temperature change of the material is due to the photothermal effect. In Fig. 5(b), the solution temperature in the GBCN system reached 61.5 °C within 100 min, further confirming the photothermal effect of GBCN. Additionally, Fig. 5(c) and (d) revealed that the use of recirculating condensate impeded the photocatalytic removal efficiency, emphasizing the crucial role of the photothermal effect in pollutant removal.

The light absorption capacity and bandgap characteristics of the samples were assessed through UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra (UV–vis DRS). In Fig. 6(a), the visible light absorption edges for BiOBr and g-C3N4 were identified at 438 and 472 nm, respectively. Upon integration with GQDs, the light absorption edge of the resulting GBCN composite extended to 483 nm. The determination of band gap energy was conducted using the Kubelka-Munk equation:
(5)
αhv
= A
hv − Eg
n / 2
(6)
E VB
= X −
E e
+ 0.5
E g
(7)
E CB
=
E VB
−
E g
where α, h, ν, A, and Eg denote the absorption coefficient, Planck's constant, optical frequency, constant, and bandgap energy, respectively. For indirect semiconductors like g-C3N4 and BiOBr with n values of 4 [20], the obtained bandgap values were 2.70 and 2.82 eV, respectively (Fig. 6(b)). The positions of the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) for both materials were calculated based on semiconductor electronegativity (X) and hydrogen-scale free electron energy (Ee). For g-C3N4, VB and CB potentials were found to be 1.58 and −1.12 eV, respectively, while for BiOBr, the corresponding values were 3.08 and 0.26 eV (Fig. 6(c)).

As shown in Fig. 6(d), photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was employed to investigate electron transfer in photocatalytic materials. The PL intensity of g-C3N4, particularly pronounced at 370 nm excitation, indicates rapid carrier complexation. In contrast, the composite GBCN shows a substantial reduction in peak intensity under the same excitation wavelength, suggesting slower carrier complexation and enhanced redox properties. However, under identical conditions, the photoluminescence intensity of BiOBr is notably weak, with no discernible signal, likely attributed to the energy band gap of the excitation light. Overall, the GBCN composite effectively mitigates photogenerated carriers, contributing to improved photocatalytic performance.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and transient photocurrent response (TPR) were employed to elucidate the migration behavior of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in photocatalysts. In Fig. 6(e), GBCN displays a smaller arc radius than g-C3N4 and BiOBr, indicating lower charge transfer resistance at the composite-electrolyte interface. Fig. 6(f) reveals that GBCN exhibits a larger photocurrent response, signifying a more effective separation of photogenerated carriers. EIS and TPR analyses collectively demonstrate that the composites facilitate efficient mitigation of photogenerated carrier complexation, thereby enhancing overall photocatalytic performance for pollutant removal.

Active species capture experiments were conducted to elucidate the pivotal active substances to the photocatalytic synergistic removal of the RhB and Cr(VI) system. Various agents, including EDTA-2Na, isopropanol (IPA), furfuryl alcohol (FFA), p-benzoquinone (p-BQ), and potassium bromate (KBrO3), were introduced to selectively trap h+, OH, 1O2, O2 −, and e−. In Fig. 6(g), IPA, FFA, and p-BQ minimally affected Cr(VI) reduction, while EDTA-2Na significantly enhanced Cr(VI) reduction by facilitating the separation of e−-h+ pairs. Conversely, KBrO3 markedly inhibited Cr(VI) reduction, indicating that e− is the main active species in the reduction reaction. In RhB degradation, KBrO3 exhibited an obvious promoting effect, while EDTA-2Na, IPA, FFA, and p-BQ led to varying degrees of inhibition, demonstrating the contributions of OH, 1O2, O2 − to RhB degradation in the coexistence system.

Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was further employed to gain insights into the active species involved in the photocatalytic synergistic removal of the RhB and Cr(VI) system. In Fig. 6(h) and (i), GBCN-300 exhibited a characteristic triple ESR spectrum under dark conditions, with a 1:1:1 intensity ratio attributed to the 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidin-1-yloxy (TEMPO) signal. Light exposure for 5 min led to a notable decrease in ESR signal intensity, indicating the presence of e− and h+. In Fig. 6(j) and (k), the use of 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) trapping agent revealed ESR quadruple spectra corresponding to OH and O2 − after light exposure [49]. 2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-4-piperidone hydrochloride (TEMP) trapping agent confirmed the presence of a characteristic 1:1:1 triplet state signal of 1O2 (Fig. 6(l)). Collectively, e− mainly contributed to the reduction of Cr(VI), whereas the oxidation of RhB was mainly attributed to h+, with OH, O2 −, and 1O2 also playing a role.

DFT calculations provided insights into the energy band structure of the material. In Fig. 7(a) and (b), the energy band structure and total density of states (TDOS) of BiOBr and g-C3N4 were depicted. BiOBr and g-C3N4 exhibited bandgap values of 2.57 and 2.41 eV, respectively, consistent with experimental results. Although the composite GBCN displayed nearly indistinguishable calculated bandgap values (Fig. 7(c)), the TDOS of the composites significantly increased compared to BiOBr and g-C3N4, indicating improved separation of photogenerated carriers [50,51]. The reduction in the composite bandgap expanded visible light absorption, facilitating enhanced carrier separation [52]. In Figs. 7(d) and 8(e), the partial density of states (PDOS) of BiOBr and g-C3N4 highlighted the orbital distribution. GBCN exhibited a negative shift in the conduction band bottom, attributed to bandgap narrowing from orbital hybridization within the composite (Fig. 7(f)) [53]. This observation aligns with trends in the energy band structure diagram. In summary, the composite promotes carrier separation, enhances charge transport, and augments the photocatalytic performance of GBCN.

Density functional theory was further employed to calculate the work functions of BiOBr, g-C3N4, and GQDs to determine the electron transfer mechanism. As shown in Fig. 7(g)–(i), the calculated surface work functions (Φ) of the BiOBr (102), GQDs (100), and g-C3N4 (001) are 5.425, 4.873, and 5.016 eV, respectively. The relationship between the Fermi energy levels and the semiconductor work function is expressed by Eq. (8):
(8)
Φ =
E vac
−
E F

Here, Evac and EF are the vacuum and Fermi energy levels and Evac is set to 0. Consequently, the EF of BiOBr (102), GQDs (100), and g-C3N4 (002) are estimated as −5.425, −4.783, and −5.016 eV, respectively. The schematic in Fig. 7(j) illustrates their EF positions before contact. GQDs serve as electron donors and acceptors, bridging electron transfer. Due to the higher Fermi levels of g-C3N4 and GQDs, electrons at the g-C3N4 conduction band (CB) move to GQDs, then migrate from GQDs to BiOBr until EF equilibrium. This electron export process forms a positive charging process, creating an electron depletion layer and bending energy bands upward. Conversely, BiOBr continuously accepts electrons, causing bands to bend downward, establishing an internal electric field (IEF) between BiOBr, GQDs, and g-C3N4, impeding electron flow (Fig. 7(k)). Under visible light, BiOBr and g-C3N4 generate excited e− in the CB position and h+ in the VB position. Driven by the Coulomb force and internal electric field, the e− accumulated at the CB position in BiOBr are rapidly transferred across the GQDs electron bridge to the VB of g-C3N4 to combine with h+ (Fig. 7(l)). This dynamic electron transfer mechanism keeps the h+ at the VB position in BiOBr and the e− at the CB position in g-C3N4 with better redox properties.

The simultaneous removal mechanism of RhB and Cr(VI) was illustrated in Fig. 8. Experimental results revealed that the ECB and EVB of BiOBr are 0.26 and 3.28 eV, respectively, while those of g-C3N4 are −1.12 and 1.58 eV, respectively. The ECB of BiOBr cannot react with O2 to form O2 −, and the EVB of g-C3N4 prevents the reaction with OH− or H2O to form OH. Thus, Type-II heterojunctions do not explain the presence of O2 − and OH. According to the analysis above, the Z-scheme heterojunction provides a more fitting explanation for the production of active substances within this system. Electrons at the CB position in g-C3N4 can react with O2 to form O2 −, while holes at the VB position in BiOBr react with OH− to generate OH. Subsequently, O2 − reacts with h+ to form 1O2, while h+ acts as an oxidizing agent. As a result, h+, OH, O2 −, and 1O2 within the system contribute to the photocatalytic oxidation of RhB, while electrons located in the CB position of g-C3N4 serve as reducing agents, reducing Cr(VI) to Cr(III).

The dynamic degradation process of RhB under varying light durations was explored using three-dimensional fluorescence, demonstrating the evolution of fluorescence centers in 3D excitation-emission matrices (EEMs). In the initial solution, primary fluorescence centers were identified at Ex/Em = 510–575/550–610 nm (Fig. 9(a)). After 30 min of dark adsorption, minimal changes in shape and position indicated limited surface adsorption (Fig. S8). Subsequent fluorescence weakening and peak shift signaled RhB breakdown, with the center blue-shifting from 550 nm to 495 nm and 575 nm to 520 nm (Fig. 9(c)). The observed blue shift is attributed to the removal of condensed aromatic groups, macromolecule decomposition, and elimination of specific functional groups, as reported in the previous study [54]. The rise in intensity reflected competing processes of N-demethylation and chromophore cleavage, contributing to elevated fluorescence. In Fig. 9(f), with extended irradiation to 100 min, peak intensity gradually diminished, indicating the further decomposition of N-demethylated intermediates into smaller molecules. Consequently, the photodegradation of RhB by GBCN photocatalysts within the synergistic degradation system was primarily governed by the disruption of the conjugated chromophore structure and subsequent mineralization, emphasizing a comprehensive degradation process rather than mere decolorization.

To further elucidate the degradation process of RhB in the coexistence system, Fukui index calculations and LC-MS were employed to analyze the vulnerable sites of RhB and the principal intermediates formed during photocatalysis. The optimized structure and atomic numbering are illustrated in Fig. S9(a). Initially, the HOMO and LUMO were utilized to identify the electron-rich and electron-deficient sites in the RhB molecule, indicating positions that are prone to oxidation and reduction reactions, respectively. In Fig. S9(b) and (c), the HOMO of RhB is predominantly concentrated near N14, designating the CN coordination as a potent oxidation site. Molecular front orbital theory and the condensed Fukui function were subsequently employed to predict the degradation pathway of RhB. The complete Hirshfeld charges and Fukui index are shown in Table S2 and Fig. S9(d). Notably, N2, N14, O23, and C23 in RhB exhibit relatively high f0 values, rendering them more susceptible to attack by reactive oxygen species. Based on these findings, the photocatalytic degradation of RhB unfolds in four stages: N-demethylation, chromophore cleavage, ring opening, and mineralization [55]. Due to its high f0 value, N14 is susceptible to ROS attacks. The removal of the N-ethyl group from the RhB molecule results in the appearance of macromolecular degradation products. Subsequently, N-ethylated chromophores (xanthene and phenyl groups) undergo ROS attacks to generate additional intermediates (P5, P6, P7, and P8). The benzene rings of P5-P8 are then targeted by active substances, resulting in the formation of ring-opening intermediates. Finally, these smaller molecules undergo further mineralization into CO2 and H2O [56].

Thirteen degradation intermediates and RhB were subjected to toxicity evaluation using the ECOSAR and T.E.S.T. programs based on quantitative structure-activity relationship. The toxicity assessment encompassed four key toxicological indicators: acute toxicity, chronic toxicity, developmental toxicity, and bioaccumulation factor, with specific values detailed in Table S4. Specifically, intermediates formed at the N-demethylation stage exhibited heightened acute and chronic toxicity compared to RhB, yet toxicity decreased in later stages (Fig. 10(a) and (b)). Small molecules from chromophore breakage and ring-opening reactions displayed negligible toxicity, such as P9 and P12. In Fig. 10(c), the high developmental toxicity of RhB and P5 was observed, yet P5 rapidly degraded, while other intermediates displayed lower developmental toxicity. Besides, intermediates generated through ring-opening and chromophore breakage exhibited a substantial reduction in developmental toxicity compared to RhB, transitioning to developmental nontoxicity. Bioaccumulation factors were assessed, with P5 showing elevated values but significant reductions observed in other intermediates, indicating decreased bioaccumulation (Fig. 10(d)). Considering that Cr(VI) was reduced to low-toxicity Cr(III) based on earlier experimental results, the GQDs/BiOBr/g-C3N4 synergistic photocatalysis effectively mitigates overall system toxicity, offering a promising approach for environmental remediation.

In this study, a novel Z-scheme heterojunction GQDs/BiOBr/g-C3N4 was proposed and applied to purifying wastewater containing coexisting heavy metal ions and organic dyes. Under visible light irradiation, a significant synergistic effect between RhB oxidation and Cr(VI) reduction was demonstrated. Within 100 min, GBCN achieved 100 % and 99.3 % removal rates for RhB and Cr(VI), respectively, showcasing 1.12 and 1.81 times higher than those in the single system. Notably, the GBCN photocatalytic system exhibited robust resistance to anionic and natural organic interferences, and was adaptable to a broad range of acid and alkaline environments. The photocatalytic efficiency of the composites was unaffected after five cycles of use, demonstrating excellent stability and reusability. A comprehensive analysis of the energy band structure and strong electronic interactions between materials revealed a robust interaction between BiOBr and g-C3N4, facilitating the effective separation of electron-hole pairs within the heterojunctions. Moreover, the samples exhibited a notable photothermal effect, improving light utilization. Additionally, GQDs served as electron reservoirs in the Z-scheme heterojunctions, further enhancing the separation and transport rate of photogenerated carriers. Results from radical trapping experiments, ESR, and work function calculations collectively affirmed that carrier transport in GBCN adheres to the Z-scheme heterojunction mechanism. Mechanistic studies identified h+, O2 −, OH, and 1O2 as the primary active substances in RhB degradation, while the highly negative conduction band potential of g-C3N4 imparted a strong reducing ability to e−, making it the dominant species in Cr(VI) reduction. The RhB degradation process was suggested as a four-phase degradation pathway: N-demethylation, chromophore cleavage, ring-opening, and mineralization. Finally, toxicity assessment indicated that the oxidized product was significantly less toxic than RhB. This study provides valuable theoretical insights for designing and preparing photocatalysts with high redox capacity for the efficient synergistic removal of the mixed pollution formed by organic dyes and heavy metal ions.
